attrs.alt: RBAC access determination flow: deny check → role collection → scope/action match

content.text: The RBAC Decision Algorithm

Every Azure control-plane request passes through a three-step evaluation before ARM responds with 200 OK or 403 Forbidden. Understanding each step prevents hours of debugging access-denied errors that look identical on the surface.

Step 1 — Deny check: if any deny assignment explicitly blocks the requested action for this principal at this scope (or a parent scope), the request is immediately rejected. No role assignment can override a deny.

Step 2 — Role collection: Azure assembles every role assignment that applies to the principal. This includes assignments to the user directly, to every security group the user belongs to (transitively), and to any managed identity associated with the request.

Step 3 — Action and scope match: Azure checks whether any collected role definition's Actions (or DataActions for data-plane) includes the requested operation, and whether the assignment's scope is at or above the requested resource. If no match exists, the request is denied implicitly.
Default is deny. Azure RBAC is additive on the allow side: permissions from multiple role assignments are unioned. A single deny assignment, however, overrides any number of allows.
Resolving the Principal: Users, Groups, and Identities
A security principal is not just a user account. ARM resolves the full set of identities that participate in a request before evaluating role assignments.
User: the Entra ID user object. Role assignments can target the user directly by object ID or UPN.
Security group: role assignments can target an Entra ID security group. All members of the group inherit the assignment. Nested groups are traversed up to the Azure-imposed depth limit.
Service principal: an application registration's service principal or a managed identity. Managed identities (system- or user-assigned) are the preferred approach for Azure workloads.

All of the above: the identity set for a user authenticated interactively includes the user object plus every group in their token. The token has a configurable group claims limit (default 150 for access tokens); overage handling is needed when group counts exceed this.

# Group membership is resolved at token issuance time.
# When a user authenticates, their token contains all group claims.
# RBAC evaluation includes all groups the user is a member of —
# both direct and transitive (nested groups up to a depth limit).

# View a user's group memberships
$userId = (Get-AzADUser -UserPrincipalName 'alice@contoso.com').Id
Get-AzADUserMemberOf -ObjectId $userId | Select-Object DisplayName, Id, '@odata.type'

# Assign a role to a group (preferred over individual user assignment)
$groupId = (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'prod-vm-operators').Id
New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId $groupId `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Operator' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/prod-rg'
Prefer group-based role assignments over individual user assignments. Groups reduce the total number of role assignments (limit: 4000 per subscription) and make access easier to audit.
Scope and Inheritance
Every role assignment targets a scope — a specific point in the Azure resource hierarchy. Role assignments propagate downward; they never propagate upward or laterally.
Scope Level
ARM Path Pattern
Inherits From
Management Group
/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<id>
Parent management groups
Subscription
/subscriptions/<id>
Management groups above it
Resource Group
/subscriptions/<id>/resourceGroups/<name>
Subscription and MGs
Resource
/subscriptions/<id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/<type>/<name>
All ancestors

A Contributor assignment at the subscription scope means Contributor on every resource group and every resource in that subscription. A Reader assignment at a resource group scope means Reader on every resource in that group but not on resources in other groups.

# Scope inheritance: a role at /subscriptions/<id> also applies at
# /subscriptions/<id>/resourceGroups/* and all resources within those groups.
# A role at /subscriptions/<id>/resourceGroups/prod-rg does NOT apply to
# resources in dev-rg or other subscriptions.

# To see all role assignments visible at a specific resource:
$resourceId = '/subscriptions/00000000.../resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm01'

# Without -Scope, Get-AzRoleAssignment returns all at all scopes in the subscription
# With -Scope, it returns only assignments AT EXACTLY that scope (not inherited)
# With -Scope -ExpandPrincipalGroups, it includes group-inherited assignments
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope $resourceId

# To see ALL effective assignments (including inherited):
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object {
    $resourceId -like ($_.Scope + '*')
}
Action Matching: Actions vs DataActions
ARM operations are split into two namespaces. Whether a role assignment covers an action depends on which namespace the action belongs to.

Actions: control-plane operations routed through ARM. Examples: Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read, Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/write. These are governed by role Actions and NotActions arrays.

DataActions: data-plane operations handled by the service itself. Examples: Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs/read, Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets/getSecret/action. These are governed by role DataActions and NotDataActions arrays.

NotActions: operations subtracted from a role's Actions. Important: NotActions is NOT a deny — it only reduces the role's own effective allow set. Another role assignment can still grant the same action.
NotDataActions: same subtraction mechanic for the data plane.

# How wildcard Actions match specific operations
# 'Microsoft.Compute/*' matches ALL Compute actions:
#   Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read
#   Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write
#   Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/delete
#   Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action
#   ... and many more

# 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*' matches only VM operations:
#   Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read  ✓
#   Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write ✓
#   Microsoft.Compute/disks/read            ✗ (different resource type)

# To see all operations for a provider:
Get-AzProviderOperation -OperationSearchString 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/*' |
    Select-Object Operation, Description, IsDataAction

# IsDataAction = True means it requires DataActions in the role definition,
# NOT Actions — these are evaluated separately at the data plane.

Control-plane access (Actions) and data-plane access (DataActions) are independent. Having Microsoft.Storage/* in Actions lets you manage the storage account configuration but does NOT let you read blobs. Blob access requires DataActions.
Checking Effective Access
ARM provides a checkAccess API that simulates the full RBAC evaluation — including deny assignments, group membership, inherited scopes, and ABAC conditions — and returns the decision.

# Check effective access for a principal at a specific scope
$upn   = 'alice@contoso.com'
$scope = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# Get all role assignments that apply to this user
# (includes inherited from groups — Get-AzRoleAssignment walks the token)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -SignInName $upn -Scope $scope | Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, Scope

# Check what a specific action resolves to for this user
# (There is no direct PowerShell cmdlet — use the ARM access check API)
$token  = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token
$apiUrl = "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/"
          + "checkaccess?api-version=2022-04-01"
$body = @{
    subject  = @{ objectId = (Get-AzADUser -UserPrincipalName $upn).Id }
    actions  = @(@{ id = 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read' })
    resource = @{ scope = $scope }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

Invoke-RestMethod -Method POST -Uri $apiUrl -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token" } `
    -ContentType 'application/json' -Body $body | Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

# Azure CLI: check role assignments for a user
az role assignment list \
  --assignee alice@contoso.com \
  --scope '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' \
  --include-inherited \
  --output table

# Check whether a user can perform an action (uses the ARM access check API)
# This requires the Microsoft.Authorization/permissions/read permission
az rest \
  --method POST \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/00000000.../providers/Microsoft.Authorization/checkAccess?api-version=2022-04-01' \
  --body '{"subject":{"objectId":"<user-object-id>"},"actions":[{"id":"Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read"}],"resource":{"scope":"/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"}}'

The checkAccess response returns a list of access decisions, each indicating whether the action is Allowed or Denied and which deny assignment or role assignment drove the decision. This is the fastest way to answer 'can this principal do this action at this scope'.
Deny Assignments in the Evaluation Flow

Deny assignments are evaluated before any allow check. They are attached by Azure itself (Blueprints, Managed Applications, Policy denyAction) — you cannot create them directly. Understanding where they come from is critical for troubleshooting.

# Check whether any deny assignment blocks an action
# Deny assignments are listed via the REST API — no direct PowerShell cmdlet
$scope = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/prod-rg'
$token = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token

$uri = "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/"
       + "denyAssignments?api-version=2022-04-01"

(Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET -Uri $uri -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token" }).value |
    Select-Object -Property name, @{ n='DenyAssignmentName'; e={ $_.properties.denyAssignmentName } },
        @{ n='DoNotApplyToChildScopes'; e={ $_.properties.doNotApplyToChildScopes } },
        @{ n='Principals'; e={ ($_.properties.principals | Select-Object -ExpandProperty id) -join ', ' } }
A deny assignment at a parent scope blocks the action at all child scopes unless DoNotApplyToChildScopes is set to true.
Deny assignments target a principal set (typically all principals, with possible exclusions for the managed application publisher).
Deny assignments do not appear in the portal's 'role assignments' view — you must use the REST API or az rest to list them.
ABAC Conditions in Evaluation

Attribute-based access control (ABAC) conditions refine a role assignment with attribute expressions. A condition is part of the assignment, not the role definition. Even when the action-scope match succeeds, the condition must also evaluate to true or the request is denied.

# ABAC conditions narrow who an allow applies to
# A condition is attached to a role assignment, not the role definition.
# Example: allow blob read only when blob path starts with 'reports/'

$condition = "@Resource[Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/containers/blobs:path]"
             + " StringStartsWith 'reports/'"

New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -SignInName     'alice@contoso.com' `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Reader' `
    -Scope          '/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/datasa' `
    -Condition      $condition `
    -ConditionVersion '2.0'

# The action match succeeds (Storage Blob Data Reader includes blob read)
# but the condition is evaluated against the request attributes.
# If blob path doesn't start with 'reports/', the condition fails => deny.
Condition Attribute Type
Example
Supported Services
Resource attributes
blob container name, blob path
Azure Storage (GA), Key Vault (preview)
Request attributes
blob content type, encryption scope
Azure Storage
Environment attributes
request time, private endpoint
Azure Storage (preview)
Principal attributes (custom security attrs)
department = 'Finance'
Entra ID with custom attrs

ABAC conditions add an 'and' clause to the allow check, not a separate evaluation pass. If the action matches but the condition fails, the result is the same 403 as if no role covered the action at all.
Troubleshooting Access Denied
A 403 Forbidden response from ARM provides an error code that guides diagnosis. The most common codes and their RBAC meaning:
Error Code
Likely Cause
First Check
AuthorizationFailed
No allow covers the action at the scope
List role assignments for the principal; verify action spelling
AuthorizationFailed (deny)
A deny assignment explicitly blocked the request
List deny assignments at the scope and parent scopes
LinkedAuthorizationFailed
The action requires write on a linked resource
Check all resource references in the ARM template
RequestDisallowedByPolicy
Azure Policy blocked the request (not RBAC)
Review policy compliance and effect; use az policy assignment list

# Troubleshoot access denied for a specific action
# Step 1: confirm the exact operation name from the activity log
Get-AzActivityLog -StartTime (Get-Date).AddDays(-1) | Where-Object {
    $_.Authorization.Action -and $_.Status.Value -eq 'Failed'
} | Select-Object EventTimestamp, Caller, @{n='Action';e={$_.Authorization.Action}}, @{n='Scope';e={$_.Authorization.Scope}}

# Step 2: check that a role covering the action is assigned at or above the scope
$action = 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action'
$upn    = 'alice@contoso.com'
Get-AzRoleAssignment -SignInName $upn | Where-Object {
    (Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name $_.RoleDefinitionName).Actions -contains $action -or
    (Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name $_.RoleDefinitionName).Actions -contains 'Microsoft.Compute/*' -or
    (Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name $_.RoleDefinitionName).Actions -contains '*'
}

# Step 3: check for deny assignments blocking the action
# (use the REST call shown in the deny assignments section above)

# Step 4: if using conditions (ABAC), verify attribute values match
# Conditions are evaluated AFTER the action-scope match; a mismatched condition
# turns an otherwise-allowed action into a deny.
Activity Log is the definitive source of truth: every ARM operation is logged with the caller identity, action, scope, and outcome. Filter on Status = Failed to find denied operations.
For group-membership issues: check that the user's token refresh has occurred after the group was added. Token caching means a newly added group may not appear in the token for up to an hour.
For DataActions issues: verify the role includes the DataActions, not just Actions. Many built-in roles grant control-plane access but not data-plane access.
For ABAC issues: check the condition expression carefully — attribute names are case-sensitive and the supported attribute set varies by service and API version.
Common Patterns and Anti-Patterns
Pattern
Recommended
Reason
Assign role to a group, not a user
Yes
Fewer total assignments; easier lifecycle management
Use the narrowest scope that satisfies the requirement
Yes
Least privilege reduces blast radius
Assign Owner at subscription scope to all engineers
No
Massive blast radius; use specific roles per resource group
Use NotActions to 'block' sensitive operations
No
NotActions is not a deny; another role assignment can re-grant
Use deny assignments to enforce security boundaries
Conditionally
Deny assignments are created by platform services; rely on Azure Policy denyAction for custom blocks
Use ABAC conditions for fine-grained data access
Yes
Avoids proliferating role assignments for different data subsets
Rely on token cache for immediate revocation
No
Revoked assignments may remain effective until the token expires (up to 1 hour)
PIM and Time-Bounded Assignments

Privileged Identity Management (PIM) adds just-in-time access on top of standard RBAC. A PIM-eligible assignment does not grant access until the user activates it. Once active, it participates in RBAC evaluation identically to a permanent assignment — the access determination algorithm is the same.
Eligible assignments: visible to the user in the PIM portal but do not contribute active permissions until activated.
Active assignments: full participants in RBAC evaluation. Created at activation time and expire after the configured duration (1–8 hours by default, configurable up to 24 hours).
Activation can require MFA, a business justification, and manager approval — all configured in the PIM policy for the role.
Emergency access ('break-glass') accounts should hold permanent, non-PIM assignments to ensure access when PIM infrastructure is unavailable.

# Privileged Identity Management (PIM) changes the assignment lifecycle.
# With PIM, a user holds an 'eligible' assignment rather than an 'active' one.
# They must explicitly activate the assignment (with optional MFA, justification,
# and approval) before the role becomes active.

# PIM activation creates a time-bounded active assignment in Entra ID.
# From ARM's perspective, an activated PIM role is indistinguishable from a
# direct role assignment — it participates in RBAC evaluation the same way.

# Check a user's eligible assignments (requires AzureADPreview or MS Graph)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagement.Read.Directory','PrivilegedAccess.Read.AzureResources'

$userId = (Get-MgUser -UserId 'alice@contoso.com').Id

# List PIM-eligible resource role assignments
Get-MgIdentityGovernancePrivilegedAccessGroupEligibilityScheduleInstance -Filter "principalId eq '$userId'"

# For Azure resource roles (subscription-level PIM)
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleEligibilityScheduleInstances?api-version=2020-10-01&$filter=principalId+eq+<user-object-id>'

PIM does not change the RBAC evaluation algorithm — it changes when an eligible assignment becomes active. ARM sees only the active assignments; eligible-only assignments are invisible to the access check.
Token Lifetime and Revocation Latency

Role assignment changes propagate to ARM within seconds — the ARM control plane is consistent. However, the caller must have a token that was issued after the assignment change. Token caching introduces a revocation gap.

# RBAC evaluation uses the caller's access token.
# Tokens have a lifetime (typically 60-75 minutes for access tokens).
# Changes to role assignments or group membership take effect at the NEXT token issuance.

# To force immediate re-evaluation (requires re-authentication):
# For CLI sessions:
az logout
az login

# For PowerShell sessions:
Disconnect-AzAccount
Connect-AzAccount

# For service principals using managed identity, the token is obtained
# from IMDS and cached per-process. Restart the process or use az account
# clear-token-cache to force refresh.

# In automation (pipelines, Functions), the token refresh happens automatically
# when the cached token nears expiry (within the token's ExpiresOn window).
# Revoked role assignments may remain effective for up to the token lifetime.

# To check the token lifetime of the current Az PowerShell session token:
$ctx = Get-AzContext
($ctx.Account.ExtendedProperties)['AccessTokenExpiry']
# Or inspect the decoded token:
$token = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token
$payload = $token.Split('.')[1]
# Pad to multiple of 4 for base64 decoding
$pad = 4 - ($payload.Length % 4); if ($pad -lt 4) { $payload += ('=' * $pad) }
[System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([System.Convert]::FromBase64String($payload)) | ConvertFrom-Json | Select-Object exp, nbf, oid, groups
Scenario
Effective Delay
Mitigation
New role assignment added
Up to 5 minutes (ARM propagation)
Wait or force-refresh token
Role assignment removed
Up to token lifetime (60-75 min)
Force re-authentication or rely on conditional access policies
Group membership change
Up to 1 hour (token claim refresh)
Force re-authentication; for critical revocation, disable the user account
PIM activation
Seconds after activation
None needed — PIM issues a fresh token
PIM deactivation / expiry
Immediate in PIM, but token still valid until expiry
Same as role assignment removal
Resource Locks vs RBAC: Where Each Fits

Resource locks are an ARM-layer guard that operates outside RBAC evaluation. They block write and delete operations for ALL principals, including Owners and Global Administrators. RBAC and locks serve different purposes and compose rather than substitute for each other.

# ARM resource locks block write and delete operations BEFORE RBAC evaluation.
# Locks are applied by any user with Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write permission.
# They affect ALL users including Owner — even a Subscription Owner cannot
# delete a resource that has a CanNotDelete lock without first removing the lock.

# Lock types:
#   CanNotDelete — read and write allowed; delete blocked
#   ReadOnly     — read allowed; write and delete blocked

# Create a lock on a resource group:
New-AzResourceLock `
    -LockName   'protect-prod-rg' `
    -LockLevel  'CanNotDelete' `
    -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' `
    -Notes 'Production guard — remove via change ticket only'

# Remove a lock (requires Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete permission):
Remove-AzResourceLock `
    -LockName          'protect-prod-rg' `
    -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' `
    -Force

# List all locks in a subscription:
Get-AzResourceLock | Select-Object ResourceGroupName, ResourceName, LockType, LockId

# Locks and RBAC interact as follows:
#   1. Lock check happens outside RBAC — it is an ARM layer guard
#   2. If a lock blocks the operation, RBAC is not consulted
#   3. If no lock applies, RBAC evaluation proceeds normally
Mechanism
Who it affects
Can Owner override?
Use case
RBAC deny assignment
Specified principals (excludable)
No (unless excluded)
Prevent specific operations by all users
Resource lock CanNotDelete
Everyone
No (must remove lock first)
Protect production resources from accidental deletion
Resource lock ReadOnly
Everyone
No
Freeze configuration during change freeze windows
RBAC role (no write action)
Principal without write role
N/A
Normal least-privilege access control
Management Group Scope and Tenant-Wide Access

Management group assignments are the broadest scope in Azure RBAC. A single assignment at the root management group effectively grants the role to every subscription in the tenant. This makes MG-level assignments powerful for platform and compliance teams but dangerous if misconfigured.

# Management group role assignments propagate through subscriptions.
# An assignment at /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/my-mg
# applies to every subscription in that management group and all child MGs.

# Platform team pattern: assign Security Reader at the root management group
# so the security team has read access across the entire tenant.
$mgId = (Get-AzManagementGroup -GroupName 'rootMG').Id

New-AzRoleAssignment `
    -ObjectId          (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'platform-security').Id `
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Security Reader' `
    -Scope             $mgId

# To see all role assignments at a management group:
Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope $mgId

# Note: management group role assignments do NOT appear when you call
# Get-AzRoleAssignment at a subscription scope without the -IncludeClassicAdministrators flag.
# You must query the management group scope directly to see MG-level assignments.

# Show effective assignments at a subscription, including inherited from MGs:
az role assignment list \
  --scope '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' \
  --include-inherited \
  --output table

Root MG assignments are invisible when listing assignments at a subscription scope without explicitly querying the MG. Always use --include-inherited or query the MG scope directly for a complete picture.
Tenant Root Group is the implicit parent of all management groups. Assignments here reach every subscription in the tenant.

Azure AD Global Administrators can elevate themselves to User Access Administrator at the root MG via 'Access management for Azure resources' in Entra ID settings — this is a one-time break-glass capability that creates a permanent assignment.
Auditing RBAC at Scale

Regular access reviews are required for most compliance frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI DSS). ARM's role assignment API provides all the data needed to generate comprehensive reports across a subscription.

# Generate a full access report: who can do what on a subscription
$subscriptionId = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
$scope = "/subscriptions/$subscriptionId"

# Get all role assignments (including inherited from management groups)
$assignments = Get-AzRoleAssignment -Scope $scope -IncludeClassicAdministrators

# Expand each assignment with role definition details
$report = $assignments | ForEach-Object {
    $roleDef = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name $_.RoleDefinitionName
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        DisplayName        = $_.DisplayName
        SignInName         = $_.SignInName
        ObjectType         = $_.ObjectType
        RoleDefinitionName = $_.RoleDefinitionName
        IsBuiltIn          = $roleDef.IsCustom -eq $false
        Scope              = $_.Scope
        Actions            = ($roleDef.Actions -join '; ')
        NotActions         = ($roleDef.NotActions -join '; ')
    }
}

# Export to CSV for access review
$report | Export-Csv -Path './rbac-access-report.csv' -NoTypeInformation

# Optionally pipe to Where-Object to find overprivileged assignments
$report | Where-Object { $_.Actions -match '\*' -and $_.Scope -eq $scope } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName
Azure Policy 'Audit usage of custom RBAC roles' and Microsoft Defender for Cloud's 'Over-provisioned identities' recommendations automate continuous RBAC hygiene without manual scripting.
Summary
RBAC evaluation is a three-step algorithm: deny check → role collection → action/scope match. Default is deny.
The principal set includes the user, all group memberships (transitive), and any managed identity.
Scope is hierarchical: assignments at a parent scope are inherited by all child resources.
Actions (control plane) and DataActions (data plane) are evaluated independently — a role must explicitly include DataActions to grant data-plane access.
NotActions is a subtraction from a single role's allows, not a system-wide deny. Use deny assignments or Policy denyAction for hard blocks.
ABAC conditions add an attribute expression to a role assignment; a failed condition produces the same 403 as no matching role.
The ARM checkAccess API and Get-AzRoleAssignment are the primary diagnostic tools; the Activity Log provides the authoritative record of denied operations.
